ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class; no specific Thai spicy-sour whole squid entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers squids and cuttlefishes as a category. No prepared Thai-style spiced squid entry exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 represents squid as a food item. No Thai-style preparation-specific code exists; closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Thai Style Spicy and Sour Whole Squid is approximately 300 g, comprising one whole cleaned squid cooked in a spicy-sour Thai sauce, served as a shared seafood dish.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150); adapted with Thai spicy-sour sauce component estimates from USDA Foundation Foods and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai-style spicy-sour whole squid exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. USDA SR Legacy cooked squid (NDB 15150, ~92 kcal/100g) was used as the primary base. Sauce components (chilli, lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, oil) were estimated using USDA Foundation Foods and FAO/INFOODS SEA data to adjust macros upward to reflect the full prepared dish. Final per-100g values represent the composite prepared dish including sauce.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; squid and shellfish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style spicy-sour whole squid. Squid and shellfish are high-protein, low-carbohydrate foods. GI is estimated at 30 based on analogous low-carbohydrate seafood preparations documented in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 300g serving = (30 x 4.5 x 3.0) / 100 = 4.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 300g serving provides approximately 1440 mg sodium, which represents 60–72% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2400 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced fish sauce when ordering.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 30 and only 4.5g carbohydrate per 100g, this dish has a low glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions without added rice or starchy sides.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol content from squid offset by low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Squid is naturally high in dietary cholesterol (~195 mg/100g) but low in saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware of the cholesterol content while noting that the low saturated fat and omega-3 profile of squid may be cardioprotective when consumed in moderation; the high sodium from fish sauce remains a concern for blood pressure management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Style Spicy and Sour Whole Squid is a Southeast Asian seafood dish featuring a whole cleaned squid cooked in a vibrant Thai-inspired sauce combining fresh chillies, lime juice, fish sauce, lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, garlic, and shallots. The dish is typically prepared by wok-frying or stir-frying the whole squid at high heat to achieve a tender yet slightly chewy texture, then coating it in the spicy-sour sauce that delivers a bold balance of heat, acidity, and umami. The squid flesh is firm and mildly sweet, contrasting with the sharp citrus and chilli notes of the sauce. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Chinese seafood restaurants as a shared table dish, often accompanied by steamed jasmine rice. Nutritionally, the dish is a high-protein, moderate-calorie preparation providing approximately 105 kcal per 100g, with 14.8g protein and only 3.2g fat, making it a lean seafood option. The carbohydrate content is low at 4.5g per 100g, resulting in a low glycemic load suitable for blood sugar management. However, the sodium content is elevated at approximately 480 mg per 100g due to fish sauce, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is considered halal. It is rich in vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and cholesterol, the latter being characteristic of cephalopod seafood.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai-style spicy-sour whole squid was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked squid with sauce component adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy squid entry or FAO/INFOODS SEA data for this food category. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 which is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for cooked squid with no hydrogenated fat components in the sauce. The food_name in the input record contained an emoji and ampersand; these have been normalised to the canonical English name 'Thai Style Spicy and Sour Whole Squid'. The cuisine tag in the input was listed as 'Chinese' but the dish is Thai-style; cuisine has been corrected to 'Thai' with Singapore as the serving context. The input cuisine_tags included 'Crab' and 'Lobster' which are not relevant to this squid dish and have been excluded. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce; this is clinically significant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Cholesterol content is characteristic of squid as a cephalopod. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI x carbohydrate_g per 100g x serving_size_g / 100) / 100 = (30 x 4.5 x 3.0) / 100 = 4.1.
